cuisine: Taiwanese origin / Singaporean bubble tea chain
region_of_origin: Taiwan (bubble tea culture, 1990s); ubiquitous in Singapore
food_subgroup: Taro-flavoured milk tea with tapioca pearls — brewed black tea combined with taro paste or taro powder, non-dairy creamer, sugar syrup, and black tapioca pearls

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for taro milk tea boba. Closest parent is tea-based sweetened milk beverage (FOODON:00002473 — milk tea). No exact URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tea-based beverages — milk tea (closest parent match in FoodEx2 matrix; no exact code for taro milk tea with tapioca pearls exists)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for taro milk tea boba. JF00AM (milk tea / tea-based milk beverage) is the closest defensible parent. Tapioca pearl component is not separately codeable in FoodEx2 at this level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for taro milk tea boba as a food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 500ml cup of taro milk tea with tapioca pearls as served at Singapore bubble tea chains such as Gong Cha, KOI Thé, and LiHo, weighing approximately 510g including tapioca pearls.

nutrient_source.title: Taro Milk Tea Boba — composite estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy components (brewed black tea, taro root, tapioca, sugar, non-dairy creamer)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (ARS) — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for taro milk tea boba as a composite beverage. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component entries: brewed black tea (FDC ID 171917), taro root cooked (FDC ID 170071), tapioca (FDC ID 169717), granulated sugar (FDC ID 169655), and non-dairy creamer powder (FDC ID 173578). Proportions are based on standard Singapore bubble tea chain formulation at full sugar (500ml serving). Singapore HPB database does not contain a specific taro milk tea boba entry. Values cross-referenced against HPB general bubble tea guidance where available.

gi_source.title: Composite GI estimate: taro (baked/boiled, GI ~54), tapioca pearls (GI ~81), sucrose (GI ~65) — weighted composite for full-sugar taro milk tea boba serving
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for taro milk tea boba as a composite beverage in the University of Sydney GI Database. Composite GI of ~76 is estimated from weighted component GIs: taro root boiled (GI ~54, University of Sydney GI Database), tapioca pearls (GI ~81, University of Sydney GI Database — tapioca pudding analogue), and sucrose/sugar syrup (GI ~65). At full sugar (500ml), total available carbohydrate per serving is approximately 94g, yielding GL ~71.6. This is one of the highest GL beverages available in Singapore. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (25mg per 100g; ~128mg per 500ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Taro milk tea with tapioca pearls poses minimal direct sodium risk for hypertension; however, chronic high-sugar intake from regular full-sugar consumption may contribute to metabolic syndrome and secondary cardiovascular risk, so patients should be counselled to reduce sugar level to 25% or 0% when ordering.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high glycemic load per serving (GL ~71.6) driven by ~50g added sugar plus ~32g high-GI tapioca starch at GI ~76
type2_diabetes.guidance: Full-sugar taro milk tea with tapioca pearls is one of the highest GL beverages in Singapore and should be avoided by patients with Type 2 Diabetes; if consumed, order at 0% sugar (reduces added sugar from ~50g to ~10g) and consider omitting tapioca pearls to reduce GL by approximately 26 units, though residual carbohydrate from the taro base and creamer remains significant.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from non-dairy creamer (0.8g per 100g; ~4.1g per serving) and potential trans fat from partially hydrogenated vegetable oils in non-dairy creamer (0.05g per 100g; ~0.26g per serving), combined with high added sugar load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit taro milk tea with tapioca pearls due to saturated fat and potential trans fat from non-dairy creamer, and the high added sugar content which promotes dyslipidaemia and insulin resistance; choosing 0% sugar, no pearls, and chains that use real taro paste over taro powder reduces overall cardiovascular risk from this beverage.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High added sugar (~42–50g per full-sugar cup) and potential trans fat from non-dairy creamer are proinflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid regular consumption of full-sugar taro milk tea; if ordering, choose 0% sugar and no pearls, and prefer chains using real taro paste (which contains anti-inflammatory anthocyanins) over artificial taro powder (nutritionally empty maltodextrin).

llm_training.natural_language_description: Taro Milk Tea with Tapioca Pearls (芋头奶茶珍珠, Yùtou Nǎichá Zhēnzhū) is one of Singapore's most popular bubble tea orders, recognisable by its distinctive purple-grey or purple-blue colour. It is made by combining brewed black tea with taro flavouring — either natural taro paste ground from actual taro root (Colocasia esculenta) or, more commonly at mainstream chains, artificial taro powder (typically maltodextrin with taro flavouring and purple food colouring) — along with non-dairy creamer, sugar syrup, and cooked black tapioca pearls. The drink is shaken or blended and served over ice in a sealed 500ml cup. The flavour profile is mildly sweet, starchy, and earthy with a creamy texture from the non-dairy creamer; the tapioca pearls add a chewy, gummy texture contrast. At full sugar, a 500ml cup contains approximately 42–50g added sugar and 32g tapioca starch, yielding a total carbohydrate load of approximately 94g per serving and a glycemic load of approximately 71.6 — among the highest of any beverage available in Singapore. The taro flavour varies significantly between chains: premium chains (The Alley, specialty cafés) use real taro paste which provides genuine nutritional value including moderate GI (~54), dietary fibre, and potassium; most mainstream bubble tea chains (Gong Cha, KOI Thé, LiHo) use cheaper taro powder which is nutritionally empty. A critical clinical counselling note for patients with Type 2 Diabetes: the sugar level ordering system allows significant carbohydrate reduction — ordering at 0% sugar reduces added sugar from ~50g to ~10g — but tapioca pearls still contribute ~32g high-GI starch regardless of sugar level selected, so pearl omission is also recommended for glycaemic management. Per 100g: 88 kcal, 1.0g protein, 1.5g fat, 18.5g carbohydrate, 9.8g sugar, 0.2g dietary fibre, 25mg sodium.
common_user_queries: taro milk tea GI glycemic index
how many calories in taro boba 500ml

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for taro milk tea boba as a composite beverage. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries for brewed black tea, taro root, tapioca, sugar, and non-dairy creamer, proportioned to a standard Singapore full-sugar 500ml formulation. GI is a weighted composite estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values. Confidence is medium rather than high because the composite estimation introduces uncertainty in exact proportions across different chains.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the non-dairy creamer and taro powder composite is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy at the composite beverage level as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite beverage as served (with ice dilution) is not reliably reported in USDA FoodData Central for this preparation. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): non-dairy creamer is plant-derived and black tea contains no cholesterol; confirmed per USDA SR Legacy component data. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no animal-derived dairy is used; non-dairy creamer contains no B12; confirmed per USDA SR Legacy. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no vitamin D-fortified ingredients in standard formulation; confirmed per USDA SR Legacy component data. trans_fat_g of 0.05 per 100g reflects potential residual trans fat from partially hydrogenated vegetable oils in non-dairy creamer; this varies by chain and supplier. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (76 × 94.35) / 100 ≈ 71.6, where 94.35g carbohydrate = 18.5g per 100g × 510g serving / 100. The 'taro' flavour authenticity varies significantly between chains: real taro paste provides anthocyanins and moderate nutritional value; artificial taro powder (maltodextrin + colouring) is nutritionally empty. Clinical counselling should address both sugar level reduction and pearl omission for diabetic patients.
